attributes.stem:
A 61-year-old woman presents with some uncomfortable skin changes around her vulva. This has been developing over several weeks and she has noticed some thickened, white areas which have become itchy and painful. The itching has become so severe that it frequently leads to scratching, impacting her sleep and overall well-being. She also experiences occasional itchiness around the anus and intercourse has now become painful. There is no history of abnormal vaginal discharge or bleeding. The patient has a history of Graves' disease, being treated with methimazole.

On examination atrophic hypopigmented mucosa are observed in the interlabial sulcus, labia minora, perineal, and perianal regions. Additionally, there are some areas of purpura and erosions over her vulva. Her vagina is unaffected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has lichen sclerosus (LS); a chronic scarring dermatological disorder that commonly affects the anogenital skin of post-menopausal women and prepubertal girls. Its exact cause is unknown, but there seems to be a possible association with other autoimmune diseases such as thyroid disorders and alopecia areata. The condition primarily occurs in the anogenital region, accounting for 85 to 98% of cases, and is characterised by intense pruritus and possibly pain or soreness. Vaginal discharge is typically absent. If the lesion extends into the anal region, it may result in a classical "figure-eight appearance" and cause pruritus ani and painful defecation. Extragenital LS can occur but is rare.

Physical examination often reveals white, atrophic papules and macules that may merge into plaques. Scratching can lead to excoriations and mild lichenification. Due to the fragility of the affected skin, petechiae with purpura and ecchymoses may be present. As the disease advances, there may be significant scarring and atrophy, leading to the loss of vulval architecture. The vagina is never affected in LS.

Vulvar lichen planus is an idiopathic inflammatory disease affecting the vulvar mucous membrane, sharing many similarities with LS. It is also more common in older, postmenopausal women. Dominant symptoms include severe vulvar pain, burning, and pruritus. In contrast to LS, 70% of cases involve inflammatory vaginitis with vaginal discharge. The condition presents with flat-topped, purple, polygonal papules, with erythema and erosions seen on the vulvar labial mucosa. Scarring can cause significant anatomic disruption, which is more severe than that in LS. Other mucosal and cutaneous surfaces, especially the oral cavity, are frequently affected as well.

Lichen simplex chronicus is a chronic, non-scarring inflammatory disease of the vulvar skin characterised by intense and unrelenting itching and scratching. Pain is not a major feature. The vulvar skin may appear thickened (lichenified) and may be erythematous due to intense scratching, but atrophic white nodules are not a common finding. The majority of patients have a personal or immediate family history of allergies or atopy.

Severe vulvovaginal candidiasis can present with chronic vulvar itching or burning. The discharge is classically described as white, thick, adherent to the vaginal sidewalls, and clumpy (curd-like or cottage cheese-like). It is not associated with atrophic white nodules on the vulva, and the vagina frequently exhibit erythema.

Psoriasis can occur in genital locations and is pruritic, but it usually appears with inflamed, well-demarcated plaques. Recognition of psoriasis involving other body sites can be helpful for diagnosis.
learningPoints: Lichen sclerosus is a chronic scarring dermatologic disorder characterised by intense pruritus and potential soreness over the vulva.
LS is identified by the presence of white, atrophic plaques on the vulva, while the vagina is almost always unaffected.
The management of lichen sclerosus typically involves the use of topical ultra-potent steroids, such as clobetasol propionate 0.05%, administered in a three-month regimen.
references: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Diagnosis and management of vulvar skin disorders: ACOG Practice Bulletin, Number 224. Obstetrics and gynecology [Internet]. 2020 Jul [cited 2023 Aug 7];136(1):e1-4. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32590722/ doi: 10.1097/AOG.0000000000003945

Kirtschig G. Lichen sclerosus—presentation, diagnosis and management. Dtsch Arztebl Int [Internet]. 2016 May [cited 2023 Aug 7];113(19):337. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4904529/ doi: 10.3238/arztebl.2016.0337
